claim: Two-way prompting (multi-step human-in-the-loop interactions) is more powerful than single-prompt workflows because it allows agents to ask clarifying questions and humans to provide structured feedback
actionable: Design agentic workflows with bidirectional prompting - have agents ask for file selection, URL input, and improvement feedback rather than requiring users to structure inputs upfront
source_title: Two-Way Prompts: SIMPLIFY your AI Agents

claim: Three tiers of agentic workflows exist: humans prompting (user initiates), two-way prompting (bidirectional back-and-forth), and fully autonomous agents (agents proactively work overnight and alert humans)
actionable: Design agentic workflows with the expectation that they will evolve from human-initiated to two-way to fully autonomous; build for the future tier while meeting current needs
source_title: Two-Way Prompts: SIMPLIFY your AI Agents

claim: Partial agentic workflows with human-in-the-loop using two-way prompts should not be avoided as they are valuable stepping stones toward fully autonomous workflows
actionable: Build and deploy human-in-the-loop agentic workflows now; don't wait for perfect autonomy when two-way prompting can deliver value immediately
source_title: Two-Way Prompts: SIMPLIFY your AI Agents

claim: The user (engineer, product builder) is the essential guiding force in agentic workflows and should remain the center of design rather than being replaced by autonomy
actionable: Keep users in the loop and empowered even as agentic workflows become more autonomous; focus on driving results for users rather than maximizing agent independence
source_title: Two-Way Prompts: SIMPLIFY your AI Agents
